bibliographic_data.title: Statistical Inference with Nonignorable Non-Probability Survey Samples
bibliographic_data.abstract: Statistical inference with non-probability survey samples is an emerging topic in survey sampling and official statistics. Much of the existing literature assumes that the participation mechanism for non-probability samples is ignorable. In this paper, we develop a pseudo-likelihood approach to estimate participation probabilities for nonignorable non-probability samples when auxiliary information is available from an existing reference probability sample. We further construct three estimators for the finite population mean using regression-based prediction, inverse probability weighting (IPW), and augmented IPW estimators, and study their asymptotic properties. Variance estimation for the proposed methods is considered within the same framework. The efficiency of our proposed methods is demonstrated through simulation studies and a real data analysis using the ESPACOV survey on the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic in Spain.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper develops a pseudo-likelihood approach to estimate participation probabilities for nonignorable non-probability samples and constructs three estimators for the finite population mean using regression-based prediction, inverse probability weighting (IPW), and augmented IPW estimators.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use auxiliary information from an existing reference probability sample to develop a pseudo-likelihood method for estimating participation probabilities under a nonignorable participation mechanism. They then construct three estimators: regression-based prediction, inverse probability weighting (IPW), and augmented IPW estimators.
outcomes: Developed a pseudo-likelihood approach to estimate participation probabilities
Constructed three estimators for the finite population mean using different methods
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper addresses statistical inference with non-probability survey samples, which is an emerging topic in survey sampling and official statistics. The authors develop a pseudo-likelihood approach to estimate participation probabilities when auxiliary information from an existing reference probability sample is available. They construct three estimators for the finite population mean: regression-based prediction, inverse probability weighting (IPW), and augmented IPW estimators. These methods are studied under asymptotic properties and their efficiency is demonstrated through simulation studies and a real data analysis using the ESPACOV survey on the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic in Spain.
